coredata.dc:title: A unique turn-off fluorescent strategy for sensing dopamine based on formed polydopamine (pDA) using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as fluorescent probe
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Weng, Shaohuang
Liang, Dong
Qiu, Huazhang
Liu, Zongen
Lin, Zhen
Zheng, Zongfu
Liu, Ailin
Chen, Wei
Lin, Xinhua
coredata.dc:description: A rapid, facile fluorescence sensing strategy was developed for dopamine (DA) detection based on polydopamine (pDA) formed on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The prepared GQDs were strongly luminescent because of the aromatic planar structure. DA could self-polymerize to pDA and snap to the surface of GQDs to form a thin film in alkaline environment, leading to quenching of fluorescence due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). With the optimized conditions, the analytical performance illustrated high sensitivity, selectivity in a wide linear range with a detection limit of 8nM. In addition, the unique method was successfully applied for DA injection test with satisfactory labeled amount. Finally, the proposed strategy was applied for DA detection in serum, wherein accurate results were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine detection
Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent sensor
Polydopamine
Serum

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimal reaction temperature and dynamics study

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the preparation of GQDs and the detection of DA.

(A) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of GQDs. (B) The emission curves of GQDs with the different excitaion wavelength. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra (inset: the fluorescence images) of GQDs with different concentrations of DA.

The AFM (A, B) and TEM (C, D) of GQDs (A, C) and GQDs with 10μM DA (B, D). (E) The FTIR spectra of the GQDs, pDA and GQDs-pDA. (F) The XPS spectra of the GQDs and GQDs with different concentrations of DA.

(A) The fluorescence intensities of GQDs with a series of 50μM interfering substance, DA and blank. (B) The fluorescence intensities of GQDs with DA in the presence of 50μM interfering substance, mixture of interferences and blank. (C) The fluorescence response of GQDs to various concentrations of DA. (D) The calibration curve of DA detected with GQDs. Error bars represent standard deviations of measurements (n =3).

(A) The fluorescence response and (B) the calibration curve of GQDs to various concentrations of DA in 10% serum. (C) The comparison of calibration curve of GQDs to various concentrations of DA in Tris-buffer and 10% serum.
Detection results of DA injection based on the proposed method.

head.ce:title: A unique turn-off fluorescent strategy for sensing dopamine based on formed polydopamine (pDA) using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as fluorescent probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Faculty of Pharmacy, The Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350108, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: 476 Hospital of PLA, Fuzhou 350002, PR China. Tel.: +86 591 88611588; fax: +86 591 88611588.

Corresponding author at: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Faculty of Pharmacy, The Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou 350108, PR China. Tel.: +86 591 22862016; fax: +86 591 22862016.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid, facile fluorescence sensing strategy was developed for dopamine (DA) detection based on polydopamine (pDA) formed on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The prepared GQDs were strongly luminescent because of the aromatic planar structure. DA could self-polymerize to pDA and snap to the surface of GQDs to form a thin film in alkaline environment, leading to quenching of fluorescence due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). With the optimized conditions, the analytical performance illustrated high sensitivity, selectivity in a wide linear range with a detection limit of 8nM. In addition, the unique method was successfully applied for DA injection test with satisfactory labeled amount. Finally, the proposed strategy was applied for DA detection in serum, wherein accurate results were obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterial is a big family containing carbon dots (C-Dots), graphene oxide (GO) nanoparticles, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [1]. As a new member of carbon-based nanomaterials, GQDs have inherited several merits from traditional carbon-based nanomaterials, such as low toxicity, biocompatibility, and photostability [1–3]. The specific excellent properties of GQD have inspired extensive studies in photovoltaic devices [4], anti-cancer drug carrier [5], and especially in bioanalysis [6] and cell imaging [7]. Compared with other carbon-based nanomaterials, GQD is superior because of its relatively higher quantum yield (QY) [3]. Recently, various studies on GQD have focused on preparation and property development, while investigations on the analytical applications are still very initial [6,8]. However, fabrication of special GQD that can both detect a target with specificity and indicate a signal response sensitively still limits its wide application.

Dopamine (DA) is an important neurotransmitter that mainly exists in the brain and central nervous system of mammals and transmits neurosignals regarding usual movements [9]. DA also plays an important role in the nervous activity, such as learning and memory [10,11]. Thus, DA could act as a biomarker for various central nervous system-related diseases, such as Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, and anorexia [12], by revealing the levels of health issues [13,14]. Facile and accurate analysis of DA could greatly benefit the field of physiological function and clinical diagnosis of special neurological diseases. In the past few years, various strategies have been developed for DA detection, including electrochemical methods [15–17], enzymatic methods [18], photo-electrochemical sensor [19], and high performance liquid chromatography [20]. However, inherent shortcomings make these methods a bit difficult to satisfy the increasing requirements for simple, reliable, and cost-efficient methods. Recently, fluorescent strategies for detection of DA have been attracting increasing interests because of its low cost, easy control, and maneuverability in biological matrixes [21–24]. However, with the need for a sensitive and selective DA sensor, especially in complex systems (e.g. serum), the development of fluorescent strategy for DA is still in its initial stage [21–23].

Dopamine, as generally known, tends to self-polymerize to polydopamine (pDA) in alkaline environment [25,26], which is highly controllable by adjusting the pH value. Interestingly, DA can snap to the surface of nearly all kinds of materials to form thin, surface-adherent polydopamine films [25], which have attracted extensive applications on coating materials [27–31], controlled release [32], and immunoassays [33]. However, studies on sensitive sensors that utilize the polymerization of dopamine for detection are rarely reported. GQDs prepared from pyrolysis of citric acid are surface-passivated and have good fluorescence activity [34,35]. The change in the surface of GQDs, such as assembling a thin layer, can induce FRET and quench fluorescence intensity to fabricated signal-off sensor [36]. Although some proposed sensors for dopamine detection are based on fluorescent route [21,23], the mechanism of DA detection based on the selective polymerization of dopamine on the surface of fluorescent nanomaterials with high sensitivity and anti-interference ability in complex system remains blank.

In this paper, we proposed a unique, selective, and sensitive signal-off fluorescence strategy for DA detection. The GQDs prepared from pyrolyzed citric acid [34,35] were used as fluorescent probes. In the absence of DA, GQDs generate a bright fluorescence with excitation at a given wavelength in an alkaline buffer solution. When DA is added, DA would be adsorbed on the surface of GQDs and becomes pDA through the self-polymerization effect of DA in alkaline condition. The formed pDA could generate a thin layer on the surface of GQDs and result to an efficient fluorescence quenching of GQDs through FRET. Based on the fluorescence quenching, the amount of DA can be effectively monitored easily and inexpensively, as shown in Scheme 1 .

Citric acid was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Dopamine (DA), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA) were purchased from Sigma. All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received. The water used throughout experiments was purified by Millipore system. The reaction buffer used in this system was Tris–HCl consisting of 10mM Tris–HCl (pH=9.5).

GQDs stock solution was prepared from the pyrolyzed citric acid (CA) according to the reported literature [34] with minor modification. In a beaker, 2g CA was heated at 200°C for 30min, and the colorless CA solids turn gradually into orange liquid to gain GQDs. Then, the orange liquid was moved to 100mL10mgmL−1 NaOH solution slowly, stirring was needed to make the solution homogeneous. Finally, the pH value of GQDs was adjusted to be 8.0. The obtained GQDs solution was used as stock solution and stored at 4°C when not used.

Various amounts of DA solutions were prepared using Tris–HCl (pH=9.5); mixed 270μL DA solutions with 30μL GQDs together and stirred well; heated the mixture at 60°C for 90min. Immediately, fluorescence measurements were taken.

All fluorescence measurements in this study were operated by a Cary Elipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Australia). The sample cell was a 350μL quartz cuvette. The excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were 390nm and 465nm respectively. Both the excitation and emission slits were set at 5nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were collected with a JEOL JEM-1400 Microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was surveyed using an ESCALAB 250XI electron spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The ultraviolet visible (UV–vis) spectrum was obtained by ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum was performed on Nicolet Avatar 360 spectrometer.

First, the optical properties of GQDs were examined by determining the UV–vis absorption and fluorescent spectra, as shown in Fig. 1 . The prepared GQDs displayed an obvious absorption peak at 365nm ascribed to π–π* transition of CC in the UV–vis spectrum [37]. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra presented in Fig. 1A show that maximum excitation and emission wavelength are 390 and 465nm, respectively. As displayed in Fig. 1B, the emission wavelength of GQDs was stable, with the excitation wavelength ranging from 300nm to 420nm, illustrating its excitation-independent fluorescent property.

The feasibility for the proposed sensors for DA detection was confirmed by the obtained UV–vis spectra and fluorescent images for different concentrations of DA in the solution. Fig. 1C reveals that the absorption value decreased regularly with the increase in DA, suggesting that DA could decrease the absorption value through some special function. Furthermore, the relationship between DA concentration and response signals was tested through the brightness of GQDs at various concentrations of DA under a UV lamp, as shown in the inset of Fig. 1C. The results showed that the blue fluorescence of GQDs was gradually reduced with the increase of DA concentration in the solution. Moreover, the results suggested that the addition of DA can effectively quench the blue fluorescence of GQDs, specifically allowing the accurate detection of DA in real samples.

DA can attach onto surfaces of solids, including nanomaterials, to form thin and uniform polydopamine (pDA) films. Thus, in such detection process, addition of DA induces its self-polymerization forming pDA, which is then be spontaneously adsorbed on the surface of GQDs, and eventually form pDA/GQDs. The formed pDA films on GQDs induce FRET and fluorescence quenching (the inset of Fig. 1C), which is like an Hg–melamine system in the GQD solution [36]. The zeta-potential of GQDs decreased from −1.62mV to −10.82mV and −18.37mV when 20μM and 100μM DA were added into GQDs solution, respectively. The zeta-potential values suggested that the significant change in the surface states of GQDs may be attributed to the introduction of DA forming pDA with the OH outward [7]. Furthermore, the topographical changes of the formed pDA/GQDs were differentiated by AFM and TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 . In the absence of DA, pure GQDs demonstrated an average thickness of about 1.2nm. Addition of DA caused significant corrugations of GQDs and the blank mica surface, which were attributed to polymerization of DA on GQDs surface and the reaction buffer. The significant topographical change was accompanied by a height increment of about 5nm. The significant increase in height suggested that polymerization of dopamine but not dopamine adsorption [21] occurred in the proposed strategy. Similarly, the TEM image revealed that the diameters of GQDs ranged from 3nm to 6nm. The diameters of GQDs obviously increased upon reacting with DA to form pDA. Moreover, Fig. 2D shows that certain number of GQDs snapped to the surface of the formed pDA to be a combinant.

FTIR spectroscopic analysis was applied to provide information on the functional groups in the formation of pDA/GQDs, as shown in Fig. 2E. The peaks at about 1722, 1592, and 1395cm−1 indicated the existence of carboxyl, CO, and CO; whereas, the peak at 1264cm−1 corresponded to the epoxy group CO [21]. The pDA had characteristic bands at 1630 and 1385cm−1, corresponding to aromatic CC bonds of indole and CNC stretching modes [38], respectively. The formed pDA/GQDs illustrated the overlapping bands of pDA and GQDs at ranges of 1730cm−1–1570cm−1 and 1420cm−1–1370cm−1, and offered the reduced bands at 1592, 1395, and 1264cm−1 of GQDs, which were attributed to the cover effect of pDA. The addition of DA to the detecting system to quench the fluorescence of GQDs through the formation of pDA on the surface of GQDs was further confirmed by XPS, as shown in Fig. 2F. Compared with the XPS survey spectrum of the original GQDs, intense signal of N1s peaks were observed in the spectra of GQDs and DA system with 50μM and 100μM DA, respectively. The atomic percentages of N were 2.52% and 3.8% for the addition of 50μM and 100μM DA in GQDs, respectively. Given that the samples for XPS analysis were purified by dialyzing for several days to remove all possible ions and small molecular residual contaminants, the observed N1s peaks were attributed to the nitrogen element of pDA. The increased content of N suggested that more pDA formed on the surface of GQDs which eventually induced high quenching efficiency. Thus, the reduced amplitude of fluorescence intensity can be applied to quantify the concentration of DA.
A series of optimization experiments were performed to gain the most appropriate conditions for the determination of DA, as shown in Fig. S1.

The GQDs served as fluorescent probe in our experiments. To obtain the best fluorescence intensity, different amounts of GQDs were tested in the optimization experiments. Stock solution of GQDs was diluted in various ratios, and the corresponding fluorescence intensities for each dilution were measured. As shown in Fig. S1A, highest fluorescence values were obtained when GQDs stock solution was diluted to a ratio of 10:1. Thus, tenfold dilution of GQD stock solution was selected as the optimum solution in the proposed strategy.

To achieve a highly sensitive determination of DA using GQDs as probe, the pH values of the reacting buffer (Tris–HCl) were studied and optimized. Fig. S1B shows that varying pH values from 6.5 to 12.5 had little effect on the initial fluorescence intensity of GQDs. This result indicated that the blank values of GQDs were unified and stable. The addition of DA led to fluorescence quenching over a wide pH range. However, the quenching efficiencies of DA at these pH values were quite different. The DA had greater quenching effect in alkaline media than in acidic solution. The highest quenching efficiency was detected at pH 9.5, suggesting that this pH value was the most suitable for the proposed strategy. This result can be attributed to an enhanced polymerization of DA to pDA and its adsorption on the surface of GQDs at pH 9.5 in such detecting system. Therefore, pH 9.5 was utilized for fluorescent sensing strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal reaction temperature and dynamics study
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of temperature on the efficiency of the changes in the fluorescence intensities before and after the addition of DA into GQDs solution was studied. As shown in Fig. S1C, the degree of quenching increased with temperature and achieved the maximum at 60°C. In addition, we studied the dynamics of the reaction at three temperature levels to optimize the reaction time for detection of DA, as shown in Fig. S1D. At a special temperature, the fluorescence intensity was gradually quenched along the reaction time tested. The reaction rates at 50°C and 60°C were higher than that at 25°C, while 60°C was considered the most appropriate. Furthermore, fluorescence intensity was quenched to a highest extent after 100min of reaction time. Thus, 60°C and 100min were selected as the optimal reaction temperature and reaction time for GQDs and DA, respectively.

Various common substances in plasma may interfere with the accuracy of quantitative detection of DA. Therefore, the selectivity and specificity of the method must be investigated. Fig. 3A shows that common substances, including AA, UA, Cys, and so on, had nearly no effect on the fluorescence intensity of the samples compared with the blank. Conversely, DA significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity of GQDs at a much lower concentration. In particular, AA and UA, which have similar electrochemical properties to DA, did not affect the selectivity of the proposed sensor. Furthermore, a competitive experiment of the DA detection with the presence of interferences was also operated to discuss the specificity of the method, as shown in Fig. 3B. It shows that the changed values of the fluorescence intensities of DA and the DA with presence of common molecules are similar, illustrating that the existing interference did not interrupt the accurate test of DA.

Considering the appreciable quenching of fluorescence intensities of GQDs brought by DA addition, the determination of DA through the proposed method was assessed. The fluorescence profile for series of concentrations of DA in the GQD solution with the detection conditions is shown in Fig. 3C. The fluorescence of GQDs at 460nm was highly sensitive to DA, and was quenched with increasing concentration of DA. The fluorescence intensity decreased when DA was increased from 0μM to 300μM. Thus, 300μM DA could completely quench the fluorescence intensity of GQDs, illustrating that the efficiency of quenching was sensitive to the concentration of DA. A plot of F 0/F versus the concentrations of DA showed a non-linear relationship in the range of 0.01μM–300μM. A linear equation (F 0/F =0.99526+0.03045× C DA (μM)) was obtained in the DA concentration range of 0μM–60μM (R =0.9977), as shown in Fig. 3D. The limit of detection (LOD) for DA was 0.008μM based on S/D=3. In addition, good precision was indicated (relative standard deviation, RSD) by 2.9%, 5.4%, and 8.5% for five independent detections of 0.5, 1, and 5μM DA, respectively, suggesting the excellent reliability of the proposed fluorescence sensing strategy. The above results illustrated that the proposed fluorescence sensor based on GQDs enabled highly favorable and accurate recognition of DA in complex samples.

Furthermore, the stability of GQDs was investigated under continuous UV irradiation and in the long term of a 6-week period, as shown in Fig. S2. Upon UV irradiation at 365nm for 150min, a 6.7% decrease in the fluorescence intensity of GQDs was observed. Moreover, the fluorescence response of GQDs was stable with only a 7.8% decrease at the end of 6-week period when stored in a refrigerator at 4°C. The results indicated the satisfactory long term stability of GQDs.

The proposed strategy was applied to test DA injection in buffer with the standard addition method and calculated the labeled amount to investigate the reliability for practical application. As shown in Table 1 , the labeled amounts of the six different DA preparations injected ranged from 92.6% to 106.8%, which satisfied the requirements of Chinese Pharmacopoeia Standards. Thus, these results further suggested that the proposed strategy was appropriate for real detection of DA injection.

Considering the complicated composition of clinical sample, the proposed method was used to detect various concentrations of DA in simulated serum samples prepared by adding fetal calf serum to buffer to a 10% final content. As shown in Fig. 4 , although the fluorescence intensity of GQDs in 10% fetal calf serum was slightly lower, a similar trend in change as presented in Fig. 3C was observed, wherein fluorescence intensity gradually decreased when DA was increased. This result suggests that the serum constituent nearly have no effect on the proposed fluorescence strategy. Moreover, a calibration curve (Fig. 4B) with a close slope to that of the standard solution was obtained (Fig. 4C). Considering all the results, the proposed strategy could be applied to DA detection in practical blood sample, which could even be expanded to diagnosis and therapy of central nervous system related diseases.

In this work, a unique signal-off strategy for DA detection was developed. Fluorescent GQDs were prepared and used as fluorescent probes. The feasibility was studied by analyzing the GQDs own peaks and UV spectrum characterization and proved to be a good one. The quenching mechanism was proposed as FRET, which was characterized through various techniques, including AFM, TEM, zeta-potential, FTIR, XPS, and so on. All characterization results had more or less verified the FRET mechanism in different respects. A good linear relationship between DA concentration and fluorescence intensity was observed, and the LOD was as low as 8nM, which satisfies the regulatory requirements. The proposed strategy exhibited high selectivity, good reproducibility, and also indicated advantages of facile fabrication, simple operation, and excellent stability. In addition, the proposed strategy was successfully applied for DA testing in DA injection and simulated serum, achieving accurate results. This design principle of signal-off fluorescence by FRET from polymerized pDA for DA detection may be extended to other fluorescent nanomaterials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful for financial support from the National High Technology and Development of China (2012AA022604), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21405016, 21305015, 21275028), the National Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2015J01043), the Major Program of Medical and Health Foundation of Nanjing Military Region (12Z39), the Young Research Project of Health department of Fujian Province (2013-1-22).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.06.093.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications
In situ growth of silver nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots for ultrasensitive colorimetric detection of H2O2 and glucose
An electrochemical avenue to green-luminescent graphene quantum dots as potential electron-acceptors for photovoltaics
Enhancing cell nucleus accumulation and DNA cleavage activity of anti-cancer drug via graphene quantum dots
Using graphene quantum dots as photoluminescent probes for protein kinase sensing
Surface coating of graphene quantum dots using mussel-inspired polydopamine for biomedical optical imaging
Fluorescent graphene quantum dots with a boronic acid appended bipyridinium salt to sense monosaccharides in aqueous solution
Detecting subsecond dopamine release with fast-scan cyclic voltammetry in vivo
Role of the mesotelencephalic dopamine system in learning and memory processes in the rat
Real-time measurement of dopamine fluctuations after cocaine in the brain of behaving rats

Simultaneous determination of salsolinol enantiomers and dopamine in human plasma and cerebrospinal fluid by chemical derivatization coupled to chiral liquid chromatography/electrospray ionization-tandem mass spectrometry
Electrochemical detection of dopamine using porphyrin-functionalized graphene
A gold electrode with a flower-like gold nanostructure for simultaneous determination of dopamine and ascorbic acid
Application of a mercapto-terminated binuclear Cu(II) complex modified Au electrode to improve the sensitivity and selectivity for dopamine detection
Selective electrochemical determination of dopamine in serum in the presence of ascorbic acid and uric acid by using a CuO nanoleaf electrode
Direct electrochemistry of dopamine on gold – Agaricus bisporus laccase enzyme electrode: characterization and quantitative detection
Visible light photoelectrochemical sensor for ultrasensitive determination of dopamine based on synergistic effect of graphene quantum dots and TiO2 nanoparticles
Monitoring of dopamine and its metabolites in brain microdialysates: method combining freeze-drying with liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry
Graphene oxide based photoinduced charge transfer label-free near-infrared fluorescent biosensor for dopamine
Competitive adsorption of dopamine and rhodamine 6G on the surface of graphene oxide
Dopamine fluorescent sensors based on polypyrrole/graphene quantum dots core/shell hybrids
Carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of dopamine as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of iron(III) ions and dopamine
Mussel-inspired surface chemistry for multifunctional coatings
A self-assembled polydopamine film on the surface of magnetic nanoparticles for specific capture of protein
In situ synthesis of polydopamine–Ag hollow microspheres for hydrogen peroxide sensing
Polydopamine coating effects on ultrafiltration membrane to enhance power density and mitigate biofouling of ultrafiltration microbial fuel cells (UF-MFCs)
A simple method for surface modification of carbon by polydopamine coating for enhanced Li–air batteries
Improved antifouling of anion-exchange membrane by polydopamine coating in electrodialysis process
Enhanced antibiofouling of RO membranes via polydopamine coating and polyzwitterion immobilization
Mussel-inspired polydopamine coated mesoporous silica nanoparticles as pH-sensitive nanocarriers for controlled release
In situ deposition of gold nanoparticles on polydopamine functionalized silica nanosphere for ultrasensitive nonenzymatic electrochemical immunoassay
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Graphene quantum dot as a green and facile sensor for free chlorine in drinking water
Graphene quantum dots as fluorescence probes for turn-off sensing of melamine in the presence of Hg2+
Strong two-photon-induced fluorescence from photostable, biocompatible nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for cellular and deep-tissue imaging
Formation of polydopamine nanofibers with the aid of folic acid

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shaohuang Weng earned his PhD degree at the Department of Chemistry, Xiamen University, in 2009. He is an associate professor in the Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Faculty of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University. His research focuses on preparation and application of nanomaterials in biosensors and the new technologies of clinical diagnosis.

Dong Liang received his MS degree in public health from Fujian Medical University in 2009. Now, he is a lecture in the Department of Health Management, School of Public Health, Fujian Medical University. His research focuses on preparation and application of some new technologies for clinical diagnosis and public health.

Huazhang Qiu received his BS degree in Pharmacy from Fujian Medical University in 2012. He is currently an MS graduate student in the College of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. His research interests are pharmaceutical analysis and fluorescence sensors.

Zongen Liu received his BS degree in Pharmacy from Dali University in 2012. She is currently an MS graduate student in the College of Pharmacy in Fujian Medical University. His research interests are drug controlled release and analysis.

Zhen Lin earned her PhD degree at the Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, in 2012. Now, she is a lecture in the Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Faculty of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University. Her research focuses on preparation and application of nanomaterials in pharmaceutical analysis. His current research interests focus on the new technologies of clinical diagnosis.

Zongfu Zheng is Associate Chief Physician in Department of Laboratory Medicine, 476 hospital of PLA. He received his BS and MS degree in Medicine from Fujian Medical University in 1992 and 2007, respectively. His current research interests include new technologies of clinical diagnosis and novel drug carriers.

Ailin Liu is a professor of Analytical Chemistry in Fujian Medical University. He acquired his PhD degree in Analytical Chemisitry from Nanjing University in 2006. His current interests include the development of biosensors and molecular biology research.

Wei Chen is a professor of Analytical Chemistry in Fujian Medical University. He received his PhD degree in Chemical Biology in 2010. His research interests are the preparation and the bioanalytical applications of nanomaterials.

Xinhua Lin is a professor of Analytical Chemistry in Fujian Medical University. He received his BS and MS degrees in Analytical Chemistry from Xiamen University, China, in 1983 and 1989, respectively. He is also a doctoral supervisor. His research interests are modified electrode and biosensors.
